data.title: Musk's Political Pivot Is A Textbook Dip-Buying Opportunity For Tesla
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) shares plunged 14% yesterday following CEO Elon Musk’s apparent pivot against U.S. President Donald J. Trump. In just one day, Musk went from being one of the strongest advocates of this administration to bringing forward strong political accusations against the sitting U.S. President (see Tweets below from the X account of Tesla’s CEO).

I think this represents a great dip buying opportunity, since Tesla’s fundamentals have not changed at all. If anything, I believe Musk is realigning Tesla’s brand with his original user base, providing a catalyst for the stock. Tesla remains a momentum stock, with a premium valuation that is in my view tied to the presence of its founder, Elon Musk. Readers who cannot stand high volatility and those who are comfortable investing only in stocks with cheap valuations would do better to avoid this stock.

How Musk’s political pivot could help “realign” Tesla’s brand

It is no secret that Tesla’s customer base tends to be high-income consumers, affluent professionals, and tech enthusiasts. Other studies also show how Tesla’s historical customer base tends to be liberal and left-leaning, with the environment as a key topic for buying an EV.

Many have noticed how Musk’s support for Trump could alienate Tesla’s customer base. This was one of the reasons the stock rallied following disappointing Q1 2025 earnings when Elon Musk announced he would refocus his efforts on Tesla away from politics.

I think this apparent pivot away from the current administration will help re-aligning Tesla’s brand with its historical customer base, which may indeed have felt alienated by Musk’s vicinity to Trump.

Ultimately, Tesla cars are always the best-rated among EVs. I think that like in any other consumer category, automotive included, product quality and competitive pricing oversee any political considerations, in the long term. Research confirms the elements that consumers look at before choosing a car include reliability, safety, and fuel efficiency. This is not to say politics cannot hinder a brand (it can!), but that as long as Musk distances himself from politics (as he is now doing), the quality and competitiveness of Tesla’s cars are what matters.

And all that is without mentioning Tesla’s other businesses, including Energy, Services, Robotaxis, and Robotics. Some of these may be even less subject to political swings, as they have a strong B2B component (Energy, Robotics) or they are tied to the existing customer base (Services).

Can Trump hinder Tesla’s future?

The only way I can see the Trump administration hurting Tesla’s business is by removing EV subsidies. This, however, is something that was already present in the “big, beautiful tax bill” that is currently to be discussed in the U.S. Senate. Musk’s apparent “feud” with Trump could at most have the effect of confirming what should have already been priced in by the market: the removal of EV subsidies. In other terms, it does not represent new negative news that warrants any correction in Tesla’s stock.

Trump has hinted at other ways to “get back” at Musk, including removing environmental subsidies. However, most of these threats do not concern Tesla directly, but rather SpaceX (SPACE) (which has significant contracts with NASA).

Personally, I also think that whatever short-term political decision Trump may take, it will be most likely cancelled in the future. This is an idea I have discussed in many of my previous macro articles, most recently arguing that Trump now has effectively no choice except pivoting on his questionable and unconventional economic policies, especially concerning trade tariffs.

Politics remains impossible to predict by definition (something I will cover in the next section), but as long as readers believe the U.S. will go back to a “normal” of being managed based on mainstream free market, capitalistic economics and without political pivots taking place every two weeks, I think entering a position in Tesla at current levels will result in outsized gains.

Risks to my thesis and why I would only enter TSLA long term

As of data available at the time of writing, Tesla’s car sales are still seeing a slump in May 2025 across China and European markets. The launch of a revamped Tesla Model Y “blurs” these results, as Tesla is still phasing out older Models. However, whether a recovery in deliveries will be swift (or happen at all) remains to be seen.

While I deem likely Trump’s unconventional policies will be overturned, either as a result of impeachment, sidelining by Congress, or legal action in courts, I may be wrong. Trump may remain in office and enact his policies well until 2028, and Tesla may suffer for a long time due to Musk’s new apparent anti-Trump political stance.

Regardless of politics, Tesla is also a volatile stock, trading at a rich premium and subject to swift changes in momentum and sentiment. Personally, I remain bullish on Tesla, but I would only enter a position if I was willing to hold for at least a decade from now.

As I have discussed in my last coverage for this company, which was highly debated and generated almost 200 comments, I think Tesla can easily grow into its (admittedly very rich) valuation. I actually think Tesla has the potential to grow much further than its current valuation, and I see a 5X-10X potential in the long term as achievable (as discussed in my stretched bullish scenarios of my previous coverage). For details on my reasoning about Tesla’s valuation, I encourage readers to refer to that article.

However, this is admittedly not a stock for everyone and I encourage readers who are wary of volatility or do not feel comfortable holding stocks at premium valuations to stay away from Tesla, even after the dip.

Conclusion

While my personal experience is in no way of statistical relevance, I have a personal anecdote to share as a conclusion to this article. I recently was shopping for a new car and tried traditional automakers such as Audi and Porsche. Tesla (or any other EV, for that matter) was not on my radar at all, but I still ended up trying a Model 3 and a Model Y out of curiosity. The only thing I can say is that trying a Tesla felt like stepping a couple of decades into the future, compared to the last models the German rivals offer today. I ended up ordering a Model Y, which I will receive later in June (what also helped is Tesla’s 0% interest financing in my base country, Switzerland - probably a sign that deliveries are indeed not so great for Q2).

Readers who follow me will know that I have been very critical about the current U.S. administration and its economic policies, which I see akin to socialism. This is despite having been a sympathizer of Trump ahead of elections in 2024. Yet, I have decided to buy a Tesla just because the car felt… better than others. This is to say, consumers end up preferring the best products in the market and political considerations may often be overblown. Not only does existing consumer research confirm this, but I have now experienced this on myself.

I remain bullish on Tesla long term and reiterate my Buy rating with a long-term investment horizon, seeing this recent dip as a great entry point.

data.title: Bio-Techne: Slow Growth Amidst Healthcare Policy, MAHA Movement
data.description:
Summary

Bio-Techne Corporation (NASDAQ:TECH) operates in the life science reagents, testing, and diagnostic space. Its products aid in drug discovery, clinical testing, and diagnosis with a focus on proteins, antibodies, and immunoassays. Primary consumers of their products include researchers in academia, biotech research companies, and the pharmaceutical industry. No customer represents more than 10% of annual sales; therefore, its business consists of a diverse set of clients both domestically and around the globe. But along with the variable clientele comes a higher risk set. Economic downturns dampen the lab testing industry as a whole, and Bio-Techne has exposure to changes in China/U.S. trade relations. Moreover, with the current White House administration’s focus on protectionist policy, lower drug prices, FDA revamps via Secretary Kennedy, and the MAHA movement, TECH will see a mixed bag of both revenue growth & deceleration as the healthcare shake-out continues. With an expensive valuation, a complex business, and a complicated future, TECH is a sell.

Background: Annual Report & Strategy

Bio-Techne has an encouraging business model: gain exposure to testing & diagnostics through strategic acquisitions, invest in high-grade “GMP” (Good Manufacturing Practice) reagents, and develop new technologies for its two segments: protein sciences and diagnostics/genomics. The protein sciences segment represented 72% of fiscal 2024 sales (as per the Annual Report), and has a wide array of solutions, including but not limited to cytokines, antibodies, tissue culture, cell selection, manual & automated protein analysis, and immunoassays. These solutions are targeted toward various diseases/illnesses, including cancer, autoimmune, diabetes, hypertension, obesity, inflammation, neurological, and kidney disease. The diagnostics & genomics segment includes advanced cell diagnostics, molecular diagnostics, and reagents within clinical settings and blood chemistry. For example, management boasts of their “ExoDx Prostate” test, which is a urine-based assay for cancer detection, with over 100,000 tests as of fiscal 2023. The list of products and applications is not exhaustive.

Roughly 43% of sales originate outside the U.S., which opens the door to international risks as well as growth. Specifically, management seeks to expand into China & India – among other developing nations – but the Trump 2.0 impact on trade relations poses a threat to said growth. Moreover, the post-pandemic macroeconomic risks in China persist, which hurts R&D spending in the Asia Pacific region. Domestically, Bio-Techne’s clientele includes academic researchers, biotech companies, and other players in the pharmaceutical industry. There has been ongoing uncertainty when it comes to scientific research funding (we could point to the current administration as a primary cause), leading to a pull-back on laboratory research ventures. As a result, TECH shares have demonstrated lackluster performance since January 2025, dropping over 30 percent.

Government Policy Headwinds

Per the “Risk Factors” section of the Annual Report, we can glean some important details regarding the macro trends hitting TECH. In particular, R&D spending, the China factor, and an FDA makeover present certain challenges to the company’s success:

“One of our strategies is to expand geographically, particularly in China, India and in developing countries, both through distribution and direct operations. This subjects us to a number of risks, including international economic, political, and labor conditions […]” (pp. 18).

Later on in the section, we come to the healthcare impact on Bio-Techne; intuitively, one might say that the policy set by the U.S. government also sets the direction for TECH performance:

“Our Protein Sciences segment products are sold primarily to research scientists at pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies and at university and government research institutions […] Our Diagnostics and Genomics segment products are intended primarily for the medical diagnostics market, which relies largely on government healthcare-related policies and funding […] Additionally, the U.S. government’s plans to manage prescription drug prices, as well as its recently announced intention to regulate lab developed tests, may impact the customers and industries we serve by increasing the cost of commercializing and/or limiting the profitability of commercialized products. All of these payor actions and changes may have a material adverse effect on revenue and earnings associated with our diagnostic products.” (pp. 18-19)

Before we draw conclusions, it is important to note that the 2024 Q4 report was released in August 2024, which means the discussion above occurred prior to Trump 2.0. Even during the Biden administration, Bio-Techne was facing some headwinds on both the prescription drug and the lab testing fronts. It’s worth drilling down on the latter, as the lab testing climate appears to change at the whims of the Executive branch. According to an April 2025 Deseret article, “Judge foils FDA plan to regulate lab-developed tests” by Lois Collins, a U.S. District Court judge struck down a Biden-era ruling by the FDA to treat laboratory tests like medical device tests. In essence, the ruling involved thousands of various lab tests that deal with – among other conditions – cancers that require LDTs (laboratory-developed tests) for identification and diagnosis. The ruling seems like an ancillary win for TECH, but the question of lab test oversight remains unanswered, as the Texas court punted the question back to the Department of Health and Human Services.

The new HHS Secretary – RFK, Jr. – has well-known qualms about the FDA (which would definitely include lab testing regulation), prescription drug prices, and healthcare research: all of which fall under Bio-Techne’s purview. The “MAHA” Executive Order dated February 13th, 2025 signifies both President Trump’s and Secretary Kennedy’s combined desire to root out the chronic disease and reduce “over-reliance on medication and treatments”. The Order also calls for more transparency across the healthcare system, and recent so-called “gold-standard” scientific research, and coupled with the May 12th “Most-Favored-Nation” Prescription Drug Pricing Order, we have a trifecta of policy events that, frankly, has already affected laboratory testing & research across the country. Plainly stated we have directives for less medication, more targeted research, and lower prices. I will take it one step further, and make the leap: HHS policy will cap any upside in TECH shares for the next 12-24 months.

Performance Through 2025 Q3

While I may see a short opportunity in TECH shares, there may very well be reason for optimism. One source of this optimism comes from the most recent earnings commentary and CEO Kim Kelderman. Even though China continues to be, in the words of the CEO, “still challenging” (per the Q3 conference call), there are anticipated tailwinds for the GMP reagents segment with high single-digit revenue growth, and double-digit revenue growth for Wilson Wolf. Although as a whole the business grew sales by 6%, there remain muted trends among academia, NIH funding, and pharma growth. Regarding the research trend in academics and the influence behind HHS, consider the quote by the CEO below:

“[…] our US academic customers are facing uncertainty around the future funding of their research projects. This can impact purchase decisions, particularly around capital equipment. Another policy shift that has been announced by the new secretary of the Department of Health and Human Services is getting a much higher priority around combating chronic disease […] Once the dust settles around the overall level of NIH funding, Bio-Techne stands to benefit from the NIH grants geared towards these diseases as our product portfolio is perfectly aligned with those research areas.”

Dissecting the Q3 conference call and the Annual Report, it seems clear that throughout 2024 and into Q3 2025, academia revenues have been and will continue to be soft. Similarly, a reduction in NIH funding would likely be a blow to biotech & pharma research activity. But it is not all bad news: management welcomes the opportunity to embrace the MAHA movement and believes the company will gain more sales from the targeted research intended by NIH. Generally speaking, there are two modes of thought: either TECH will be at the forefront of HHS research (drawing more sales via testing and diagnostics in an attempt to fight chronic disease), or shares of TECH will lose out along with the pullback in R&D spending across academia and pharmaceuticals/lab testing. For the time being, I expect the latter.

Valuation, and Risks to a Short

There is still growth happening at Bio-Techne, and revenue growth – no matter how small – hurts a short position. Yet from 2023 to 2024, TECH saw its EBIT drop nearly 13% from $281 million to $245 million (as per Seeking Alpha). Moreover, through Q3 2025 the trailing twelve-month EBIT stands at $224 million. While the revenues are going up, earnings are going down. From this, we can infer there is a downtrend with the business: China represents 9% of the company’s revenues and still presents a macro problem. The changes going on at HHS will continue to drag on Bio-Techne’s market opportunity. The Wilson Wolf acquisition mentioned above will be completed by 2027, and although the future subsidiary is looking at double-digit growth for 2025, TECH is obligated to pay 4.4x sales for the rest of the company. It’s a double-edged sword, in that Wilson Wolf will contribute positively in the gene & cell therapy space, yet the better they do, the more TECH has to pay, up to a cap of $1 billion. At $48/share, TECH itself is trading at a 6.2x forward price/sales multiple, and a 20.6x forward EV/EBIT multiple…both are overpriced compared to medians of 3.3x and 14.6x, respectively. At a 4.4x sales multiple – roughly the same value management is paying for Wilson Wolf – TECH should be trading around $34/share.

While I see a potentially 29% downside to TECH shares, I am willing to admit – again – that sales are growing, albeit slowly, and the Wilson Wolf acquisition could pay off in the long run (the deal should be completed by 2027). Either of these outcomes or a more disciplined approach to expenses on the part of management, could ruin a short position. Yet, I believe the short risks are mitigated by the overwhelming trends away from academic research spending and toward specifically targeted healthcare policy (i.e., MAHA). Management believes they will be the winner once the healthcare dust settles, as more testing could be required to take aim at chronic disease and childhood diseases. I understand there are competing cross-currents of growth versus deceleration. For better or worse, I take the deceleration view.

Conclusion

Bio-Techne operates in a complex space and has a decent business model. There are thousands of applications for its protein segment, and the diagnostic testing space may have room to grow over time, as the Wilson Wolf acquisition plays out. Yet, TECH shares are trading at an expensive valuation based on both revenues and earnings, implying room to the downside. The macro trends are almost certainly cause for concern, as foreign growth – China in particular – remains subdued. The “Make America Health Again” movement will shake up the sector for at least the next 12 to 24 months, affecting academic research & pharma investment, and in turn, hitting Bio-Techne’s growth avenues. While the company may continue to see single-digit growth, it will be capped by policy and international headwinds.

Manager Commentary

“As our base case, we expect risk assets to experience continued positive returns in the coming quarters, with economic growth remaining resilient.”

Harbor Multi-Assets Solutions Team

Market in Review

The first quarter of 2025 saw a return of volatility to financial markets, which resulted in mixed investment results across risk assets. Following the S&P 500 Index’s new all-time high midway through the quarter, those gains reversed, and the index posted its worst quarterly performance relative to the MSCI World ex-US Index in 16 years. U.S. equities experienced a sharp correction, as valuations that had become extended were coupled with investors’ new focus on the administration’s policy agenda and the resulting negative, near-term impact on economic growth and inflation.

The S&P 500 Index returned –4.3% during the quarter — its worst quarterly performance since the third quarter of 2023 — and the Russell 1000® Index returned –4.5%. Small caps continued to lag their larger peers with the Russell 2000® Index returning –9.5%. Investors’ concerns about a potential economic slowdown, in addition to uncertainty around the new administration’s economic policies, dampened consumer confidence and investor sentiment and weighed on investment returns. The quarter was marked by a reversal in market leadership within global equities, as international equities outperformed domestic equities, while U.S. Information Technology stocks, particularly the Magnificent 7, underperformed the broader equity market. Questions surrounding “American exceptionalism” and concerns about the impact of DeepSeek’s cost-efficient artificial intelligence (“AI”) model led investors to question the profitability driving the significant investment spending behind AI. A rotation away from U.S.-centric technology leadership took place during the quarter, with growth opportunities broadening internationally. Sentiment toward non-U.S. equities reached a low point, highlighted by the extreme valuation discount versus domestic equities.

Performance data shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Past performance is net of management fees and expenses and reflects reinvested dividends and distributions. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and when redeemed may be worth more or less than their original cost. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Current performance may be higher or lower and is available through the most recent month end at harborcapital.com or by calling 800-422-1050.

Shares are bought and sold at market price not net asset value (NAV). A fund’s NAV is the sum of all its assets less any liabilities, divided by the number of shares outstanding. Market price returns are based upon the closing composite market price and do not represent the returns you would receive if you traded shares at other times.

Portfolio Performance

During the first quarter, the Harbor AlphaEdge Small Cap Earners ETF (“ETF”) returned –6.99% (NAV), slightly underperforming the Harbor AlphaEdge Small Cap Earners Index, which returned –6.92%, and outperforming its benchmark, the Russell 2000® Index, which returned –9.48%.

The ETF outperformed the benchmark as investor preference for higher-quality shares — more specifically, companies reporting positive earnings — were favored during the quarter. The ETF had positive relative performance versus the benchmark within the Information Technology and Financials sectors, as stock selection within each of the sectors was strong within the strategy. The ETF’s overweight to Financials contributed to relative performance with small-cap Financials outpacing the broader index. The ETF’s underweight to Information Technology shares also contributed to relative performance versus the benchmark as Information Technology shares lagged the broader Russell 2000® Index.

Outlook

Harbor’s Business Cycle Regime indicator continues to signal late-cycle conditions with market sentiment remaining in risk-seeking mode at the end of the first quarter. The presidential election catalyzed an increase in consumer and business sentiment, with hopes of imminent tax cuts and deregulation driving expectations for forward growth higher. Despite the new administration’s stimulative policies, there were other factors, such as slowing immigration, unclear trade policies, and a focus on reducing government spending from elevated levels, which are acting as headwinds to economic growth. Outside the U.S., pro-growth policy changes have come out of Europe, particularly Germany, where policymakers have relaxed their self-imposed “debt brake” to allow themselves the leeway to make needed investments in areas such as defense. Following 100 basis points of interest rate cuts in 2024, the U.S. Federal Reserve (“Fed”) meeting in December acknowledged that interest rate cuts would not be a given at upcoming meetings, reflecting the uncertainty around both the effects of the policies of the new administration and the terminal rate in this cycle. Fiscal policy and tariff decisions will be key determinants on whether the Fed will deliver on the two rate cuts that have been priced into the market. Inflationary pressures within the economy have continued to decelerate, with Services (Ex-Shelter) the only subsector that is meaningfully above its pre-COVID trend. Higher tariffs pose an upside risk to inflation, and we remain focused on changes in tariff policy and their effects on growth and inflation. As our base case, we expect risk assets to experience continued positive returns in the coming quarters, with economic growth remaining resilient.

Attribution Disclosures

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company. Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period.

Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Alpha is a measure of risk (beta) adjusted return.

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share (EPS) is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

% EPS Growth - Past 3 Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity (ROE) is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.

data.title: VanEck Crypto Monthly Recap For May 2025
data.description:
Ethereum (ETH-USD) rebounded in May on key upgrades, Solana (SOL-USD) gained momentum from institutional treasury moves and protocol changes, while Hyperliquid cemented its dominance in DeFi trading.

Digital assets, especially digital asset equities, outperformed in May as equity capital markets funded 12+ “crypto treasury companies” via SPAC, reverse takeover, IPO, or Secondary. Many of these companies then bought Bitcoin or other digital assets, leading to reflexive upside. For the month, the MarketVector Global Digital Assets Equity Index (MVDAPP) rose 19%. The MarketVector Smart Contract Leaders Index (MVSCLE), which tracks the largest and most liquid digital assets, is up 12%. Bitcoin +8% to a record high of $111.8K, vs. the Nasdaq +8% and S&P 500 +5%.

Price Returns

MarketVector Decentralized Finance Leaders Index 13 -50

Source: Bloomberg as of 5/31/2025. Index performance is not representative of fund performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities named herein.

Though BTC punched to a new all-time high, within the Layer 1 ecosystem we track closely, only 14 of the 35 tokens gained in the month. Altcoins were paced by a reanimated Ethereum (+45%), which re-established a credible narrative for value capture and an improved user experience. ETH’s outperformance resulted in a substantial (+27%) BTC/ETH ratio retracement as it moved from 0.019 to 0.024 over May. Despite this tremendous pullback, ETH/BTC returned to roughly the same levels it traded in mid-March.

ETH/BTC Finally Rallies: +27% in May

Source: Artemis XYZ as of 6/2/2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities named herein.

Zooming out, ETH/BTC has been in a downward trend since September 2022 after peaking amid the implementation of EIP-1559, which instilled faith in ETH as a monetary competitor to BTC due to its introduction of deflationary mechanisms. Since that apex in sentiment, however, migration to L2s reduced demand for ETH, causing the asset to become net inflationary. However, May saw a return to the “ETH is money” narrative, this time from institutional treasuries instead of retail podcasters, bringing new credibility and demand. More on Ethereum’s comeback below.

Smart Contract Platform (SCP) Fundamentals

The MarketVector Smart Contract Leaders Index (MVSCLE) Gained 17% in May but Lagged behind Majors as Long-Tail Altcoins Struggle

Despite Gaining 17% In May, MVSCLE Is Outpaced by Majors as Long-Tail Altcoins Underperform

Source: MarketVector as of 5/31/2025. The MarketVector Smart Contract Leaders Index (MVSCLE) is designed to track the performance of the largest and most liquid smart contract assets. Index performance is not representative of strategy performance. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities named herein.

Daily average DEX Volumes returned in a big way in May and were up (+111%) across all SCPs compared to April’s figures, totaling $502B in May vs $238B in April. The largest contributor to the surge in on-chain trading activity was BNB Chain, which took the top spot from Solana with an average trading volume of $6.4B per day in the month of May. BNB Chain’s total was more than double that of the next best, Solana’s, at $3.0B per day.

The large surge in trading activity contributed to the increase of onchain revenues by (+36%) month-to-month. Amongst all SCPs, Hyperliquid seized the top spot and generated more than $2.3M in average daily revenue. Hyperliquid’s topline success in May, with revenues up (+65%), has been reinforced by a series of new trading products and features launched in May, detailed further below.

Ethereum Reawakens and Gains Corporate Treasury Adoption

ETH’s performance versus BTC in May came amid the implementation of Ethereum’s long-awaited Pectra upgrade, ecosystem-wide security initiatives, and newfound interest in Ethereum as a treasury asset.

The Pectra upgrade, launched on May 7, 2025, marked Ethereum’s largest protocol update since the Merge, incorporating 11 EIPs to boost usability, scalability, and institutional staking. Account abstraction (EIP-7702) allows Externally Owned Accounts to execute smart contract logic, enabling gasless transactions, ERC-20 fee payments, transaction batching, social recovery, and single-step token approvals, simplifying DeFi interactions, cutting gas costs, and easing onboarding by removing the need to hold ETH for gas. Expanded validator limits (EIP-7251) raise the maximum effective balance to 2,048 ETH, reducing institutional staking costs by up to 98% through fewer nodes. Enhanced blob throughput (EIP-7691) and dynamic blob capacity (EIP-7742) increase Layer 2 (L2) scalability by expanding blob capacity, improving data availability, and lowering L2 transaction fees. These upgrades align Ethereum with the needs of complex applications and professional validators. Ethereum’s roadmap now focuses on execution layer improvements, like transaction parallelism and security, to further enhance throughput and DeFi scalability.

On May 14, the Ethereum Foundation announced the Trillion Dollar Security Initiative, a multi-pronged effort to elevate Ethereum’s security posture across the stack: UX, wallet security, smart contract security, cloud infrastructure, consensus and protocol security, and more. The initiative aims to build trust at a “civilization-scale,” envisioning billions of individuals comfortably storing $1,000+ onchain, plus public and private institutions storing $1T+ inside a single smart contract or application. Its launch reinforces Ethereum’s push to become the credible infrastructure for institutional and sovereign adoption, supported by the $32.6M in ecosystem grants awarded in Q1.

This revitalized infrastructure narrative coincided with a wave of institutional interest in ETH, not just as a utility token, but increasingly as a strategic reserve asset. Most striking was Sharplink Gaming (SBET), which on May 27th announced an expected $425M in private placement proceeds and named Ethereum co-founder Joseph Lubin as Chairman of the Board (pending closing of the offering), formally adopting ETH as its core reserve asset and pledging to operate validator infrastructure. The stock surged over 400% on the news. Earlier in the month, on May 15th, French real estate search engine Entreparticuliers.com (Euronext: ALENT) declared its transformation into an “Ethereum Treasury Company”, citing ETH’s commodity status, staking yield, foundational role in DeFi, and institutional adoption. After shifting towards DeFi and RWA tokenization in April, the move further anchored the French company’s corporate strategy in ETH, its stock rallying more than 800% in the days following the announcement. Both Sharplink and Entreparticuliers.com follow BioNexus Gene Lab Corp (BGLC), a small Malaysia-based biotech whose March announcement made it the first Nasdaq-listed company to commit to an exclusively ETH treasury strategy publicly. After calling a cyclical bottom expanding its Ethereum holdings in April, on May 14th, Ethereum infrastructure company BTCS announced agreements to issue up to $57.8M in convertible notes exclusively for ETH purchases, officially adopting an Ethereum treasury strategy likened to MicroStrategy’s Bitcoin treasury strategy. While still nascent compared to BTC or even SOL treasury adoption, the market’s response to these moves may reflect early signs that ETH’s monetary premium is being recognized again by equity markets.

Solana’s MicroStrategy Moment and Protocol Overhaul

Solana’s SOL token had an admirable month, gaining (+6%) as trading activity returned to the chain. DEX Volumes increased (+41%) from $66.5B to $93.7B as memecoins like WIF (+34%) and SPX (+70%) climbed back to prices not seen since January, possibly reflecting trader optimism for a return to a speculator’s market on the heels of Bitcoin all-time highs.

As we noted in April, the first shoots of a “MicroStrategy-for-Solana” movement began to sprout, with several companies publicly declaring SOL as a strategic treasury reserve asset. This early interest grew into a broader trend in May, with DeFi Development Corp expanding its holdings to over 609K SOL (~$107M), while Upexi surpassed $100M in total exposure by accumulating both spot and locked tokens. DeFi Development Corp also invested part of its SOL treasury into dfdvSOL, a liquid staking token (“LST”) delegated to the company’s own validators. Mirroring other LSTs on Solana and Ethereum enables the company to earn staking rewards while maintaining liquidity. On May 1st, Classover Holdings (KIDZ) became the latest publicly traded company to adopt a Solana treasury strategy, announcing a $400 million equity facility to fund the long-term purchase, staking, and validator operation of SOL tokens as part of its core balance sheet strategy.

Meanwhile, Sol Strategies (OTCPK:CYFRF), previously Cypherpunk Holdings, bought $18M of Solana tokens with the first tranche of the $500M note announced in April. The company also filed a $1 billion base shelf prospectus in May, signaling its intent to become the most capitalized Solana-native public vehicle to date. This move underscores how equity markets are opening up to SOL exposure in ways that mirror the early Bitcoin treasury era.

Solana’s performance in May was also driven by significant protocol upgrades and ecosystem growth, reinforcing its capabilities as a high-performance monolithic blockchain and ambitions for greater institutional applications. The Alpenglow consensus overhaul, proposed on May 19th by Solana-focused research & development studio Anza, would replace Solana’s legacy consensus mechanisms, Proof of History and Tower BFT, with Votor and Rotor, slashing deterministic finality from 12.8 seconds to 100–150 ms, a ~100x improvement. Such improvements in latency de-risk high-value applications like trading and payments on Solana, underscoring Solana’s ambition to compete for institutional-grade financial services.

Echoing Solana’s institutional focus, on May 23, the Solana Attestation Service (SAS) launched, offering verifiable credentials (e.g., KYC, accreditation) at the wallet level. This identification framework enables sophisticated compliance, access control, reputation systems, and identity-based applications to be programmed without revealing sensitive data onchain. On the consumer front, the Solana Seeker smartphone, Solana’s second mobile device, announced shipping in August. The launch introduces the SKR token and TEEPIN (Trusted Execution Environment Platform Infrastructure Network) framework for decentralized app distribution. SKR will power Solana Mobile’s economy, indicating an effort to compete with Helium’s recent success in the mobile network space. Separately, TEEPIN aims to provide decentralized authentication of hardware, software, and network layer integrity so that mobile users can access blockchain-based apps without traditional gatekeepers and fees, such as the Apple App Store’s 30% fees.

Solana also became the first non-EVM chain to integrate Chainlink’s CCIP v.16 this month, tapping into simplified cross-chain DeFi applications for $19B+ of eligible cross-chain assets.

Sui Rocked by $223M Cetus Exploit

Sui's TVL Reached New All-Time Highs Just Before the Cetus Hack

On May 22nd, Sui’s largest decentralized exchange, Cetus, suffered a $223M exploit after an attacker used fake tokens to exploit flaws in the Cetus’ smart contracts, draining its liquidity pools. Having just reached an all-time high above $2.1B, Sui’s total value locked (TVL) dropped $590M overnight as users rushed to withdraw capital from the ecosystem. The attacker was able to launder approximately $60M, but Cetus successfully froze $162M of stolen funds by halting the affected smart contracts. These funds remain immobilized on-chain as the protocol works with the Sui Foundation and blockchain security firm OtterSec on a recovery plan.

To recover the funds, a Sui governance vote initiated on May 27th proposed a one-time protocol upgrade to forcibly transfer the frozen assets from the attacker’s wallets to a multi-signature wallet co-managed by Cetus, the Sui Foundation, and OtterSec. It would mark one of the largest “hack-the-hacker” interventions in DeFi history if passed. However, the vote has reignited debates around decentralization and governance-based reversibility, reminiscent of Ethereum’s 2016 DAO hack response. In that case, a vulnerability in the DAO smart contract allowed an attacker to siphon 3.6M ETH, prompting Ethereum developers to initiate a contentious hard fork that reversed the exploit. This act ultimately split the community and gave rise to Ethereum Classic.

As in the case of Ethereum versus Ethereum Classic, it appears that the side of the white hat hackers will win. At the time of writing, 90.6% of SUI-denominated votes favor the governance proposal, with most other votes abstaining. Interestingly, Stakefish, a provider of staking services since 2018, voted no on the proposal, drawing criticism on X.

At the time of writing, SUI’s price is trading at $3.54, down 15% from its high of $4.16 on the day of the hack. Sui has recovered ~$225M (15%) of TVL from its post-hack low of ~$1.5B.

May’s Notable Performer: HYPE (+87%)

Hyperliquid Achieved 86% of Perps Volume in May; Up from 16% One Year Earlier

The biggest winner in May amongst SCP tokens was Hyperliquid’s HYPE (+65%). We discussed Hyperliquid’s potential in our December monthly amid HYPE’s startling price performance of (+154%) in the last month of 2024. Hyperliquid’s journey from a perpetual futures decentralized exchange to a more general-purpose financial chain has been noteworthy for its strong execution. Hyperliquid has been able to translate the high value of its airdrop, now worth >$10B, into a highly successful ecosystem.

As liquidity begets liquidity, Hyperliquid’s performance can partially be attributed to its ability to respond to community feedback to drive trading volumes. Hyperliquid added LAUNCHCOIN, a token central to the Believe ecosystem, on May 14th, enabling up to 3x leverage long & short perpetuals on the viral creator token launchpad. Due to popular demand, Hyperliquid also increased leverage limits to 5x on SOPH and NXPC. These new perpetuals markets helped drive Hyperliquid’s record-breaking metrics in the month, with the protocol achieving a record $5.4M in 24h trading fees on May 22nd and all-time high open interest of $8.9B. Perp trading volume on Hyperliquid reached ~$261B in May, an increase of 896% year-over-year from May 2024, driving the platform’s share of perps volume from 16% to 86% over the same period. The addition of native SOL & FARTCOIN deposits, withdrawals, and spot trading further increased the platform’s gravity amongst leading L1 & memecoin assets. The HIP-3 improvement proposal went live on Hyperliquid’s testnet to further grow its ecosystem's reach. By incentivizing the platform’s decentralized userbase to add additional perp trading pairs, the initiative could further advance Hyperliquid’s growth into new markets.

Hyperliquid ended the month with the launch of Hyperdrive, a stablecoin money market tailored for its blockchain. Hyperdrive allows users to lend or borrow stablecoins and stake $HYPE, unlocking up to 3x leverage for the yield-bearing HLP tokens, which represent deposits in Hyperliquid’s market-making and liquidation vault, earning a share of the platform’s trading fees.

On the regulatory front, Hyperliquid Labs responded to the CFTC’s requests for comment on perpetuals and 24/7 trading. Hyperliquid argues that its L1’s 24/7 trading operations demonstrate DeFi’s well-suitedness to crucial aspects of market operation, and that perpetual derivatives offer valuable use cases when implemented onchain for reasons including their transparency, programmability, and self-custody. As a leading decentralized exchange, Hyperliquid is well-positioned to gain further market share against centralized incumbents like Coinbase if favorable DeFi regulations emerge.

Disclosures

Index Definitions

S&P 500 Index: is widely regarded as the best single gauge of large-cap U.S. equities. The index includes 500 leading companies and covers approximately 80% of available market capitalization.

Nasdaq 100 Index: is comprised of 100 of the largest and most innovative non-financial companies listed on the Nasdaq Stock Market based on market capitalization.

MarketVector Centralized Exchanges Index: designed to track the performance of assets classified as 'Centralized Exchanges'.

MarketVector Decentralized Finance Leaders Index: designed to track the performance of the largest and most liquid decentralized financial assets, and is an investable subset of MarketVector Decentralized Finance Index.

MarketVector Media & Entertainment Leaders Index: designed to track the performance of the largest and most liquid media & entertainment assets, and is an investable subset of MarketVector Media & Entertainment Index.

MarketVector Smart Contract Leaders Index: designed to track the performance of the largest and most liquid smart contract assets, and is an investable subset of MarketVector Smart Contract Index.

MarketVector Infrastructure Application Leaders Index: designed to track the performance of the largest and most liquid infrastructure application assets, and is an investable subset of MarketVector Infrastructure Application Index.

MarketVector Digital Assets 100 Large-Cap Index: market cap-weighted index which tracks the performance of the 20 largest digital assets in The MarketVector Digital Assets 100 Index.

MarketVector Digital Assets 100 Small-Cap Index: market cap-weighted index which tracks the performance of the 50 smallest digital assets in The MarketVector Digital Assets 100 Index.

data.title: Tesla: Trump/Musk Feud And Robotaxi/FSD Risks Suggest More Downside Ahead
data.description:
I last wrote about Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) back in April 2024. At that time, I was very bullish as the stock was trading for just about $140 per share. Since then, the shares have more than doubled in value, and because of that major valuation change and because of some new emerging risk factors, I believe it is time to take profits and wait for a much better entry point. At current levels, I do not see a favorable risk/reward ratio. With this in mind, let's take a closer look at Tesla:

The Chart

As the chart below shows, Tesla shares have recovered significantly from the lows of around $217, that it reached in April. However, the stock plunged on June 5th, when a feud between President Trump and CEO Elon Musk erupted on social media, causing the stock to plunge by about 15% that day.

The 50-day moving average is around $291 and the 200-day moving average is nearly $305, so the stock still appears to be facing a bearish trend with more potential downside to come. This is based on a very public feud with President Trump, as well as some other potential risk factors. I think this stock could easily test the $250 range soon (where it might find some support) and possibly re-test the April lows if cooler heads don't prevail. I would consider starting a small position if the stock trades around $250 per share, but I would not buy more aggressively, unless it re-tests the April lows.

The Trump/Musk Feud

The feud all seemed to start shortly after Elon Musk left his role at DOGE. Almost as soon as he left, Elon Musk pushed back on President Trump's "Big Beautiful Bill" due to concerns that it was not cutting government spending and he even called it a "disgusting abomination." President Trump responded with posts on his own social media platform and suggested that government contracts and subsidies with companies that Elon Musk runs, could be cancelled as that would be an easy way to cut government spending. The feud seemed to escalate from there with Elon Musk stating he would have SpaceX (SPACE) decommission the Dragon spacecraft that recently rescued astronauts.

While Tesla shares plunged by about 15% in real-time as these barbs were exchanged, I think this all could get much worse in the coming days and weeks for Tesla shareholders. Being on the bad side of the Trump Administration could unravel the entire gain, if not more, that Tesla shares have seen since the April lows. I believe there is a real risk that the fallout from this feud is going to get bigger in time and lead to cancelled contracts and subsidies and it could also potentially lead to investigations and new regulations for Tesla. It may also potentially lead to the denial of approvals for things like full self-driving cars, or "FSD."

While many investors might seek to use a 15% drop in the stock as a buying opportunity, I think it is too early and not enough of a pullback. Tesla shares rallied recently because it finally seemed as though Elon Musk was going to get back to focusing on his companies and leave DOGE. But, one of his June 5th posts suggests he is in for the long-haul when it comes to politics. In one post, Elon Musk posted that President Trump has about 3.5 years left, but that he has the next 40 years.

I believe this is a major potential risk factor for Tesla shareholders to consider. This seems to be a warning to Republicans to side with Musk and vote against Trump's "big beautiful bill." This suggests that Musk is planning to remain a player and potential "Kingmaker" with political donations in American politics, especially since he even raised the idea of creating a 3rd political party. Now it seems like leaving DOGE is not going to result in Elon Musk getting back to focusing on his companies, but rather that he intends to "be around" in terms of being a player in politics for the next 40+ years, as he stated below:

"Oh and some food for thought as they ponder this question: Trump has 3.5 years left as President, but I will be around for 40+ years..."

Robotaxi Launch And FSD Risks

Tesla is scheduled to do a soft launch of its robotaxi service in Austin, Texas on or around June 12th. While it sounds promising, I believe that Alphabet's (GOOG, GOOGL) Waymo is way ahead of the game when it comes to autonomous driving. I am bullish on Alphabet, and as I wrote in this recent article, Waymo is growing rapidly and it has an impressive safety record.

Tesla's FSD technology, on the other hand, has some safety concerns including whether or not conditions like sun glare could cause an accident. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration or "NHTSA" has an ongoing investigation regarding the safety and reliability of Tesla's FSD system, in part because of serious accidents. I personally prefer Waymo's strategy which is to rely on multiple sensors as well as cameras which creates redundancy and therefore possibly more safety, whereas Tesla relies primarily on cameras to see as a human would.

Macro Risks Loom As Well

In a recent social media post, Elon Musk said that tariffs could create a recession later this year and I agree with this. I recently wrote this article outlining the reasons why the stock market appears overvalued and could drop significantly due to a recession. I believe a potential recession is looming and needed in order to bring rates down (thereby making the debt service payments for the U.S. Government more manageable). A recession is another potential risk factor that investors should consider when buying any stock, but especially one that is as richly valued as Tesla. Recessions tend to reduce profits as well as P/E multiples, so the combination of lower earnings and multiple contraction, could take Tesla shares down considerably.

Brand Damage Remains

Elon Musk's foray into politics and his role at DOGE have caused many U.S. Democrats and persons with similar political beliefs in other nations to turn on the Tesla brand. I see weekend protests at my local Tesla dealership and it is easy to spot bumper stickers on Tesla cars that say things like "I bought this before we knew Elon was crazy." Elon Musk's departure from his DOGE role may have tempered some of the more moderate "Tesla haters" but I think it is going to be a while before the brand damage fades. I think it could take many years for some consumers to forget about the support that Elon Musk gave to President Trump and for the layoffs at DOGE. However, now that Elon Musk is feuding with President Trump and even calling for his impeachment, there is a chance some Democrats will start to like Elon Musk and the Tesla brand again.

Who Benefits From This Feud?

I think this feud is very bad for President Trump and Elon Musk and probably even worse for Tesla. When two powerful and wealthy individuals are trying to take each other down and do so in public, the damage could be significant and long-lasting. However, this feud could benefit Waymo and Alphabet, as the Trump Administration could view them more favorably and even speed up Waymo approvals to expand into other cities. Amazon (AMZN) founder Jeff Bezos has aspirations when it comes to space, and he might be considered as an option for some government space contracts in the future.

Where I Could Be Wrong

There is a chance that President Trump and Elon Musk will soon realize that they are hurting themselves and their mutual causes, which could lead to some kind of detente. I remember when President Trump called Marco Rubio "Little Marco" in the past and now he is Secretary of State, so there is some hope for reconciliation. If Tesla successfully launches its robotaxi service soon and the rollout goes well, this could boost the share price.

What makes me most bullish about Tesla in the long run is the potential for humanoid robots and that is the main reason I would want to get back into the stock. Analysts at Morgan Stanley (MS) project that the market for humanoid robots could reach $5 trillion by 2050. If investors continue to focus on this potential, the pullbacks in the share price might be more limited than expected.

In Summary

I think it is clear that there are significant headline risks with Tesla and there has potentially been long-term brand damage done. The posting made by Elon Musk during his new feud with President Trump suggests he is going to remain heavily involved with American politics which could continue to create additional brand damage. Furthermore, President Trump and his administration could make life very difficult in terms of regulations, approvals, subsidies, contract awards and investigations of Tesla.

I feel that Tesla's robotaxi launch might not go as well (or be as timely) as planned, and I see Waymo as being far ahead. I am also concerned about the valuation of Tesla, since the stock price has doubled since my last article, and for all these reasons, I would sell it now. I believe there will be another chance to buy in at lower levels, and it might also make more sense to consider this stock towards the end of President Trump's term since a new administration might be more favorable.
